---
title: "4.14 Binary Search"
subtitle: "Searching Algorithms"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Binary Search

### Sorted data required

If your array or list is already in order, binary search will on average find an element or determine that it is missing much more quickly than a linear search.

Binary search can only be used if the data is sorted.

It keeps dividing the sorted search space in half.

## Binary Search Steps

### Left, right, middle

Binary search compares a target value to the value in the middle of a range of indices.

If the value is not found, it looks again in either the left or right half of the current range.

Each time through the loop it eliminates half the values in the search area until the value is found or there is no more data to look at.

## Middle Index and Bounds

### Source explanation

Binary search calculates the middle index as `(left + right) / 2`.

`left` starts at `0`.

`right` starts at the array length minus `1`.

If the target is less than the middle value, set `right` to `middle - 1`.

If the target is greater than the middle value, set `left` to `middle + 1`.

If `left` becomes greater than `right`, return `-1`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: binSearch`

The code for `binarySearch` below is from the AP CSA course description.

A recursive version of this algorithm will be covered in lesson 4.17.

## SearchTest Binary Source

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class SearchTest
{
    public static int binarySearch(int[] elements, int target)
    {
        int left = 0;
        int right = elements.length - 1;
        while (left <= right)
        {
            int middle = (left + right) / 2;
            if (target < elements[middle])
            {
                right = middle - 1;
            }
            else if (target > elements[middle])
            {
                left = middle + 1;
            }
            else
            {
                return middle;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] arr1 = {-20, 3, 15, 81, 432};
        int index = binarySearch(arr1, 15);
        System.out.println(index);
        index = binarySearch(arr1, -20);
        System.out.println(index);
        index = binarySearch(arr1, 432);
        System.out.println(index);
        index = binarySearch(arr1, 53);
        System.out.println(index);
    }
}
```
:::

## binSearch Test Target

### Runestone expected output

```text
2
0
4
-1
```

## Binary Search with Strings

### Use `compareTo`

You can also use binary search with a `String` array.

When searching for a `String`, use `compareTo` rather than `<` or `>`.

`compareTo(String other)` returns:

- a negative value if the current string is less than `other`
- `0` if the strings have the same characters in the same order
- a positive value if the current string is greater than `other`

## BinSearchStrings Source

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class BinSearchStrings
{
    public static int binarySearch(String[] elements, String target)
    {
        int left = 0;
        int right = elements.length - 1;
        while (left <= right)
        {
            int middle = (left + right) / 2;
            if (target.compareTo(elements[middle]) < 0)
            {
                right = middle - 1;
            }
            else if (target.compareTo(elements[middle]) > 0)
            {
                left = middle + 1;
            }
            else
            {
                return middle;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String[] arr1 = {"apple", "banana", "cherry", "kiwi", "melon"};
        int index = binarySearch(arr1, "cherry");
        System.out.println(index);
        index = binarySearch(arr1, "apple");
        System.out.println(index);
        index = binarySearch(arr1, "melon");
        System.out.println(index);
        index = binarySearch(arr1, "pear");
        System.out.println(index);
    }
}
```
:::

## binSearchStrings Test Target

### Runestone expected output

```text
2
0
4
-1
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

- state that binary search requires sorted data
- update `right` when target is less than the middle value
- update `left` when target is greater than the middle value
- return `middle` when the target matches
- use `compareTo` for `String` binary search

## End

### 4.14 Binary Search

Next: Search runtimes.
